modules.name: Life
modules.description: Conway's Game of Life on a 24×24 grid (B3/S23 rule). Each tick, every cell updates based on its 8 Moore neighbours. Right-click presets include alternate cellular-automaton rules: HighLife (B36/S23), Day & Night (B3678/S34678), majority rule, voter model. Outputs total live count, generation count, oscillation/blinker gate, and polyphonic per-row activity.

modules.name: Schelling
modules.description: How can mild personal preferences produce dramatic group segregation? Schelling's 1971 model proved that even a *weak* same-type preference — agents content with being a 30 % minority — produces a fully segregated city when the dynamics play out. Macro-segregation does not require strong individual prejudice. On a 24×24 grid with two populations + empty cells, unhappy agents relocate; over a few minutes the random mix sorts itself into solid red and blue blocks. Outputs segregation index, unhappy fraction, average local same-type fraction, and a segregated gate.

modules.name: Turing
modules.description: How does a leopard get its spots? Alan Turing's 1952 reaction-diffusion mechanism — the foundation of mathematical biology's account of pattern formation. Two chemicals u (substrate) and v (autocatalyst) diffuse and react across a 2D field; tiny initial perturbations grow into stable patterns. Different feed-rate F and kill-rate k pairs produce spots (leopard), stripes (zebra), labyrinth, or moving solitons. The same equations describe coat patterns, chemical clocks, sand-dune ripples, and certain ecological systems — pattern from no master plan, just local rules.
